children.name: Oriolus
children.description: The typical orioles — brilliantly coloured passerines of the Old World tropics. Males are striking combinations of yellow, black, and crimson; females are more subdued greenish or brownish. Orioles are arboreal, feeding on fruit and insects in the forest canopy. Their fluting, melodic whistles are among the most beautiful bird songs.

children.name: Oriolus oriolus
children.description: The only oriole breeding in Europe. The male is brilliant yellow with black wings and tail; the female is greenish-yellow with streaked underparts. Its rich, fluting song is unmistakable — a liquid 'weela-wee-oo' that carries through the canopy. Summer visitor to southern Sweden, inhabiting mature deciduous woodland. Winters in sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Oriolus kundoo
children.description: Formerly considered a subspecies of the Eurasian Golden Oriole. Breeds from Central Asia through the Indian subcontinent. Smaller than its western counterpart, with more yellow in the tail and a wider black eye-stripe.

children.name: Oriolus chinensis
children.description: A widespread Asian oriole with a diagnostic broad black nape-band. Brilliant yellow body with black wings and tail. Occurs from India and China through Southeast Asia to the Philippines, Sulawesi, and Java.

children.name: Oriolus xanthornus
children.description: A striking oriole of South and Southeast Asia with a black head and throat contrasting with golden-yellow body and rump. Inhabits open woodland, gardens, and plantations. Common and easily detected by its mellow whistled song.

children.name: Oriolus traillii
children.description: A spectacular oriole of Himalayan and Southeast Asian forests. Deep maroon body contrasting with a glossy black head and bright yellow bill. Inhabits evergreen and mixed deciduous forests from the Himalayas to Indochina and Taiwan.

children.name: Oriolus tenuirostris
children.description: A close relative of the Black-naped Oriole with a longer, finer bill. Ranges from the eastern Himalayas to Vietnam. Prefers montane evergreen forests. Distinguished by its thinner bill and more golden-yellow nape.

children.name: Oriolus auratus
children.description: The African counterpart of the Eurasian Golden Oriole. Very similar in appearance but with a longer bill and more yellow in the tail. Ranges across sub-Saharan Africa in moist woodland and savanna. A migratory species, moving with the rains.

children.name: Oriolus larvatus
children.description: A common and widespread African oriole with a black head, yellow body, and olive-green back. Found from Ethiopia and Sudan south to South Africa. Inhabits woodland, savanna, and gardens. Its song is a rich liquid whistle.

children.name: Oriolus brachyrynchus
children.description: A forest-dwelling oriole of West and Central Africa. Similar to the African Black-headed Oriole but with a shorter bill and more extensive black on the head. Inhabits lowland rainforest, where it keeps to the canopy.

children.name: Oriolus sagittatus
children.description: The most widespread oriole in Australia, ranging from coastal Queensland to Victoria and west to the Northern Territory. Olive-green upperparts, pale underparts with fine streaking, and a reddish eye. Inhabits open eucalypt forest and woodland.

children.name: Oriolus flavocinctus
children.description: A large, greenish-yellow oriole found from the Lesser Sunda Islands through New Guinea to northern Australia. Olive-green above, yellow below, with a strong reddish bill. Inhabits mangroves, monsoon forests, and riverine woodland.

children.name: Sphecotheres
children.description: Figbirds — a small genus of three species restricted to Australasia. Unlike typical orioles, figbirds are highly social, gathering in noisy flocks to feed on figs and other fruit. They have a more robust build and a bare patch of facial skin around the eye, giving them a distinctive appearance.

children.name: Sphecotheres vieilloti
children.description: The most widespread figbird, found in northern and eastern Australia, New Guinea, and the Lesser Sundas. Males have olive-green upperparts, yellow underparts, and a bare bright red face patch. Females are brownish with heavy streaking. Highly social, often seen in noisy flocks in fruiting trees.
